_comment:
GILFOYLE AUTONOMOUS CREW (5 stages) via the subagent (crew) tool. Runs the gilfoyle skill chain AFTER the human gate: probe (prove-it-prototype) -> design (falsifiable-design) -> plan (budgeted-plan) -> build (checkpointed-build + tdd-scoped) -> gate (final integration check + router). interrogated-spec is NOT a stage: it needs human sign-off, so the orchestrator runs it first and only submits this crew once <rundir>/spec.md carries a verbatim sign-off. The engine substitutes only {task} (= the signed spec path) into prompt_template; forward depends_on edges do NOT pipe predecessor text (stages read <rundir>/*.md on the shared cwd). The ONE exception is the loop edge (gate -> build), which injects the gatekeeper's feedback into build as context.

RUN DIRECTORY: every handoff file lives in the run directory = the directory that CONTAINS {task} (e.g. if {task} is '.inbox-unread-count/spec.md', the run dir is '.inbox-unread-count/'). Each stage derives it as dirname({task}); the prompt_templates say so explicitly so five agents don't infer it inconsistently.

LOOP POLARITY IS INVERTED FROM dotnet-test-creator, ON PURPOSE, AND THE A/B DISCRIMINATOR IS 'ORACLE-ON-INPUT', NOT 'binary == oracle'. In test-creator, any validate failure loops back to implement to self-heal. In gilfoyle, checkpointed-build step (f) says a gate failure STOPS and surfaces to the human, because 'decisions about which thing is wrong (implementation vs oracle vs design) are not yours to make from inside the executor', and tdd-scoped warns 'if you revise the oracle to match the implementation, you have lost.' A plain implementation bug ALSO makes the binary disagree with the oracle, so 'binary != oracle' cannot by itself tell a behind-spec impl from a falsified claim. The correct discriminator runs the oracle ON THE FAILING INPUT and compares to the check's EXPECTED output:
  * CLASS A (implementation is behind the spec -> self-heal, loop): a unit test or stress fixture is red AND oracle(failing input) == the check's expected output (the checks agree with ground truth; only the code is behind) AND the fix stays within the current slice's planned file set. The gatekeeper fires loop_to back to build with categorized feedback (token NEEDS_WORK). This is the 'run until a claim is falsified' state: keep working.
  * CLASS B (a claim is FALSIFIED -> HALT_FALSIFIED, no loop): oracle(failing input) != the check's expected output (a check or claim contradicts ground truth); OR the rebuilt binary drifts from the oracle in a way no single-slice edit resolves (needs a design/architecture change); OR any falsifier in the design fires; OR a regression fence encodes a design-level contradiction; OR probe != oracle at probe time (substrate suspect); OR a Class-A failure that does NOT converge (persists across the loop bound, or needs edits to files outside the current slice's planned set). The stage naming HALT_FALSIFIED names which leg (impl/oracle/design/substrate) is implicated + evidence, and the orchestrator surfaces it to the human, who decides which leg is wrong.

HALT PROPAGATION for forward-stage falsifications: probe and design can also falsify (probe!=oracle; cheapest falsifier fails). Because forward edges don't pipe text, a forward stage that falsifies writes the token HALT_FALSIFIED to <rundir>/status.md and emits it. Every downstream stage's prompt begins by reading <rundir>/status.md; if HALT_FALSIFIED is present it does nothing but re-echo it via summary(terminal). The halt thus cascades to the end of the crew and the orchestrator reports it instead of pretending the run succeeded.

WHY build is ONE stage (not one-per-slice): budgeted-plan sets slice count at runtime, so slices can't be enumerated as DAG stages. build walks all slices itself, running the per-slice gates (unit test / stress fixture / oracle re-run / budget / regression fence) INLINE in its own turns, self-healing Class-A misses inline with bounded retries. gate is the crew-visible loop point: it runs checkpointed-build's FINAL integration check (every oracle + every falsifier + every regression fence against the assembled binary) and routes. Loop bounds: trigger >= 4 chars, max_iterations 1..=10, no self-loops, planned upfront. If the loop bound is exhausted with Class-A work still open, the gatekeeper escalates to HALT_FALSIFIED (reason=non-convergence) rather than letting the crew return a silent changes_needed. Polyglot: stages use the project's own build/test/probe commands.
task: <<path to the signed interrogated-spec, e.g. .inbox-unread-count/spec.md — MUST already contain a verbatim requester sign-off; the orchestrator refuses to submit this crew otherwise>>

stages.name: probe
design
plan
build
stages.prompt_template:
Run checkpointed-build (with tdd-scoped inside each slice) for the feature specified at: {task}

RUN DIRECTORY = the directory that contains {task} (dirname of the spec path); '<rundir>/' refers to it.

FIRST read <rundir>/status.md; if it contains HALT_FALSIFIED, do nothing except call summary(resultType='terminal') re-echoing the halt reason.

FIRST RUN: read <rundir>/plan.md, <rundir>/design.md and the probe/oracle. Critique the plan before coding. Then implement slices ONE AT A TIME, in order. For each slice: impact-analysis + helper-search first; implement via RED->GREEN->BUDGET->ORACLE->REFACTOR; then run the per-slice gates INLINE — unit tests, the stress fixture, REBUILD the binary and re-run the prove-it oracle against it, check the loop/wall budget, run the slice's regression fence.

ROUTE each gate failure by the ORACLE-ON-INPUT test, not by 'binary == oracle' alone: run the oracle on the failing input and compare to the check's EXPECTED output. CLASS A (self-heal inline): a unit test or fixture is red AND oracle(failing input) == the check's expected output (the checks match ground truth; only the code is behind) AND the fix stays within THIS slice's planned file set (from plan.md). Self-heal with bounded retries (build 3x, test 5x); never skip/ignore a test; never edit the oracle or the check's expected value to pass. CLASS B (STOP the slice, write HALT_FALSIFIED to <rundir>/status.md naming the implicated leg + evidence, and stop) when ANY of: oracle(failing input) != the check's expected output (a check or claim contradicts ground truth); the rebuilt binary drifts from the oracle in a way no single-slice edit resolves (needs a design/architecture change); a regression fence fails; the fix requires editing files OUTSIDE this slice's planned set; or the slice needs a decision the plan didn't make. Commit one slice per gate-green (message references the design claim). Append a per-slice result block to <rundir>/status.md.

LOOP RE-ENTRY (gatekeeper triggered NEEDS_WORK): its categorized feedback is provided as context — read it plus <rundir>/status.md and address ONLY the named Class-A gaps; do NOT redo slices already committed green. Never modify the oracle or the design from here — those are human decisions; if the feedback implies the design/oracle is wrong, or a fix would reach outside the slice's planned files, write HALT_FALSIFIED instead of looping further.

stages.name: gate
stages.prompt_template:
Run checkpointed-build's FINAL integration check for the feature specified at: {task}

RUN DIRECTORY = the directory that contains {task} (dirname of the spec path); '<rundir>/' refers to it.

FIRST read <rundir>/status.md; if it already contains HALT_FALSIFIED, call summary(resultType='terminal') re-echoing it (a forward stage or build already halted). Otherwise: REBUILD the assembled binary and re-run EVERY prove-it oracle, EVERY falsifier in the design's Falsification table, and EVERY regression fence. Confirm each slice was committed with gates green per <rundir>/status.md.

ROUTE via the built-in summary tool, using the ORACLE-ON-INPUT test (NOT 'binary == oracle' alone): for any failing check, run the oracle on that input and compare to the check's expected output.
  * CLASS A only — a slice/fence is red AND oracle(failing input) == the expected output (checks agree with ground truth, code is merely behind) AND a single-slice edit can fix it: call summary(resultType='changes_needed') with the token NEEDS_WORK followed by a categorized list — FAILING: (build/test/fence errors), MISSING: (uncovered claims), BUDGET: (over-budget loops). The engine re-runs build with this as context. This is the self-heal path.
  * CLASS B — a claim is FALSIFIED: oracle(failing input) != the expected output (a check/claim contradicts truth), OR the rebuilt binary drifts from the oracle in a way no single-slice edit resolves, OR any falsifier fires, OR a fence encodes a design-level contradiction. Call summary(resultType='terminal') with the token HALT_FALSIFIED, name which leg (impl/oracle/design/substrate) is implicated, and give the disagreeing items as evidence. Do NOT loop — the human decides which leg is wrong.
  * NON-CONVERGENCE — if this is a loop re-entry and Class-A work still remains that repeated build passes are not resolving (or that would require edits outside a slice's planned files): call summary(resultType='terminal') with HALT_FALSIFIED, reason='did not converge after N iterations', listing what remains. Do NOT keep consuming loop iterations silently.
  * ALL GREEN: call summary(resultType='terminal') with a final report (claims shipped, fences in CI, negative space, tracker refs). Do NOT write feature code or edit oracles.
